elements.sent-2411-001: We study the problem of collaborative best-arm identification in stochastic linear bandits under a fixed-budget scenario.
elements.sent-2411-002: In our learning model, we consider multiple agents connected through a star network or a generic network, interacting with a linear bandit instance in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-003: The objective of the agents is to collaboratively learn the best arm of the given bandit instance with the help of a central server while minimizing the probability of error in best arm estimation.
elements.sent-2411-004: For this purpose, we devise the algorithms MaLinBAI-Star and MaLinBAI-Gen for star networks and generic networks respectively.
elements.sent-2411-005: Both algorithms employ an Upper-Confidence-Bound approach where agents share their knowledge through the central server during each communication round.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our algorithms enjoy exponentially decaying probability of error in the allocated time budget.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, experimental results based on synthetic and real-world data validate the effectiveness of our algorithms over the existing multi-agent algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-008: The multi-armed bandit problem (MAB) is a classic framework in sequential decision making, capturing the exploration-exploitation trade off faced in many real-world domains, for example, recommendation systems \cite{ gentile2014online, li2010contextual,li2016collaborative , clinical trials \cite{durand2018contextual, wang1991sequential , online advertising \cite{tao2018best , adaptive routing \cite{awerbuch2008online and so on.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: An instance of MAB problem consists of a set of possible choices called arms.
elements.sent-2411-010: The learning agent sequentially chooses an arm and receives a reward related to the chosen arm.
elements.sent-2411-011: The goal of the agent is to either maximize the cumulative reward (equivalently, minimize the regret) over the time, referred as regret minimization problem \cite{bubeck2012regret,cesa2013gang,lattimore2020bandit or, to identify the best arm within a specified constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The latter variant is known as the best-arm identification or pure exploration problem, which is studied in two different settings based on the specific constraint: (1) fixed-budget \cite{audibert2010best,bubeck2009pure, karnin2013almost and (2) fixed-confidence \cite{chen2017towards,garivier2016optimal, mannor2004sample .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: While the fixed-budget setting aims to identify the best arm with smallest error probability within the allocated time budget, the goal in fixed-confidence setup is to identify the best arm with the given confidence level using minimum arm pulls.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we study fixed-budget best-arm identification in stochastic linear bandit (SLB) \cite{auer2002using, abbasi2011improved .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In SLB setup, the arm set is a subset of $\mathbb{R ^d$ and pulling an arm yields a reward which is a noisy observation of the linear combination of pulled arm and an unknown vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The best arm is defined as the arm vector closest to vector $\theta$ in $\mathbb{R ^d$ space.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Various real-world scenarios can be effectively represented using the framework of stochastic linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, in online advertising \cite{tao2018best where, each advertisement option can be modeled as an arm representing the properties like genre, keywords, duration and so on.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The common vector $\theta$ can encapsulate the characteristics of the targeted audience such as age group, geographic locations, etc. In many such applications, it is appealing to use collaboration and communication in order to speed up the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, in web recommendation applications, increased amount of data and high volume of user requests overwhelms a single processor's capacity to handle learning tasks efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-021: In such scenarios, it is desirable to deploy multiple servers such that each user request is routed to one of the servers.
elements.sent-2411-022: These servers can then collaborate by sharing their local insights to collectively improve the recommendation process.
elements.sent-2411-023: Such applications have motivated the development of multi-agent collaborative algorithms where, multiple autonomous agents work together to solve a common learning task (see, for example, \cite{do2023multi, ghosh2022multi,he2022simple, SW08, XJHTZ22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: Recently, \cite{wang2023pure developed a federated algorithm for pure exploration in linear bandits under fixed-confidence framework.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, the problem of collaborative best-arm identification in linear bandits under fixed-budget setting still remains unaddressed.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we address this open problem by introducing two novel algorithms MaLinBAI-Star and MaLinBAI-Gen for linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our framework involves a communication model where a group of agents is interconnected through a communication network, collaboratively learn the best arm facilitated by a central server.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, we devise the algorithm MaLinBAI-Star designed for star networks, where $M$ independent agents perform the learning task, exchanging their observations through a centralized server.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, real-world applications often involve agents that are geographically dispersed, making it impractical to establish a central server at a location that can facilitate efficient communication for all agents simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-030: Consider, for instance, an e-commerce platform selling multiple brands (arms) and trying to determine the best-performing brand for a product \cite{shahrampour2017multi .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The platform's users (agents) may be scattered across different regions, so deploying a single server to serve all users is inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-032: To address this, the company can deploy multiple servers across various locations, with each server dedicated to collecting feedback from users within a specific geographic area, and later, the company aggregates this feedback to make a decision about the best brand.
elements.sent-2411-033: Motivated by such applications, we devise algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen which extends the applicability of MaLinBAI-Star to more complex generic networks.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen takes into account the structure of the communication network using dominating sets~\cite{haynes2013fundamentals .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Given the learning network, MaLinBAI-Gen first partitions the network into dominating sets.
elements.sent-2411-036: Each component in the dominating set partition executes MaLinBAI-Star independently where, the corresponding dominant node plays the role of central server for that component.
elements.sent-2411-037: Later, the information gathered at these dominant nodes is aggregated by an ensemble to decide the best-arm of the given bandit instance.
elements.sent-2411-038: By incorporating the network structure in the learning process, MaLinBAI-Gen manages to achieve same performance level as of MaLinBAI-Star while reducing the communication cost significantly.
elements.sent-2411-039: Notably, finding the minimum dominating set of a graph is computationally hard.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, there is a well-established body of research offering approximation algorithms for computing the minimum dominating set, as discussed in \cite{haynes2013fundamentals, kuhn2003constant, wieland2001domination .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Nevertheless, the upper bound for the error probability of our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen, remains independent of the size of the dominating set.
elements.sent-2411-042: In fact, MaLinBAI-Gen is designed to function efficiently with any valid dominating set partition, making it adaptable across a range of network structures.
elements.sent-2411-043: Related work: Fixed-budget best arm identification in linear bandits has been studied previously for single agent setting \cite{alieva2021robust,hoffman2014correlation, katz2020empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-044: BayesGap \cite{hoffman2014correlation uses a Bayesian approach which incorporates the correlation among the arms using a Gaussian process.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Peace \cite{katz2020empirical uses a experimental design based on the Gaussian-width of the underlying arm set.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Recently, \cite{azizi2021fixed proposed the first BAI algorithm for generalized linear models.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In \cite{yang2022minimax , authors developed a minimax optimal algorithm OD-LinBAI which uses the G-optimal design for arm selection.}
elements.sent-2411-048: A related work in \cite{wu2016contextual uses a adjacency graph to capture the dependency between users where, observed rewards on each user are determined by a group of neighboring users in the graph.}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, none of these algorithms incorporate collaborative learning.
elements.sent-2411-050: Recently, \cite{wang2023pure proposed a federated algorithm to tackle the pure-exploration problem in linear bandits within a fixed-confidence framework.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Nevertheless, there remains a significant gap in the literature of linear bandits regarding federated pure exploration under a fixed-budget setting.
elements.sent-2411-052: In this paper, we fill this gap by introducing two collaborative algorithms MaLinBAI-Star and MaLinBAI-Gen, designed for fixed-budget best arm identification.
elements.sent-2411-053: Another area of research focuses on collaborative regret minimization \cite{kolla2018collaborative, liu2010distributed, szorenyi2013gossip, zhu2023distributed, karpov2024communication .}
elements.sent-2411-054: A significant body of work has been devoted to collaborative regret minimization specifically for stochastic linear bandits (SLBs).
elements.sent-2411-055: Most closely related work on federated learning in linear bandits include \cite{amani2023distributed,do2023multi, dubey2020differentially,ghosh2022multi, he2022simple, huang2021federated, li2022asynchronous,wang2019distributed, chawla2022multiagent addressing the problem of collaborative regret minimization.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In particular, \cite{he2022simple,li2022asynchronous considers the centralized model with asynchronous communication between the server and agents.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The work in \cite{dubey2020differentially establishes a decentralized learning algorithm with deferential privacy.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Recently, \cite{amani2023distributed established a lower bound on the communication cost of distributed contextual linear bandit and proposed a minimax optimal algorithm for regret minimization.}
elements.sent-2411-059: However, the primary focus of these studies is regret minimization, which does not directly tackle the specific pure exploration problem that we aim to address.
elements.sent-2411-060: The communication model used for generic networks in our algorithm is close to \cite{kolla2018collaborative where, the authors used the concept of dominating set partition to incorporate the network structure.}
elements.sent-2411-061: While \cite{kolla2018collaborative deal with the problem of collaborative regret minimization, recently \cite{jha2022collaborative extended their work to multi-agent best arm identification under fixed budget.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, both of those papers focus on classic multi-armed bandits and can not be directly applied to linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-063: With this paper, we add to this literature and propose the collaborative best-arm identification algorithm for linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-064: Main Contributions: Our main contributions are the following:.
elements.sent-2411-065: We devise an algorithm MaLinBAI-Star for fixed-budget best arm identification in star networks.
elements.sent-2411-066: Building on this, we generalize the approach to arbitrary network topologies, introducing the algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen.
elements.sent-2411-067: This algorithm doesn't make any assumption on the network structure except that the network should remain consistent over the execution time.
elements.sent-2411-068: MaLinBAI-Gen integrates the network's structure into the learning process through dominating set partitions, delivering error bounds similar to those achieved by MaLinBAI-Star while incurring lower communication costs.
elements.sent-2411-069: Further, we show that the upper bound for error probability of our algorithms is near-optimal with respect to the lower bound presented in \cite{yang2022minimax .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Finally, we conduct extensive numerical experiments on both synthetic and real-world data and compare our algorithms to several baselines including (1) minimax optimal single-agent fixed-budget pure-exploration algorithm OD-LinBAI \cite{yang2022minimax , (2) multi-agent regret minimization algorithms FedLinUCB \cite{he2022simple , Async-LinUCB \cite{li2022asynchronous and, (3) multi-agent fixed-confidence best-arm identification algorithm FALinPE \cite{wang2023pure .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The numerical results demonstrate that our approach stands out notably in contrast to the aforementioned algorithms.

elements.sent-2411-081: Furthermore, we assume that $\forall a \in \mathcal{A , \|a\|_2 \leq L$ and $\|\theta\|_2 \leq W$ for some constants $L$ and $W$.}

elements.sent-2411-087: In order to determine the best arm, agents need to sequentially estimate the unknown vector $\theta$ using their observation history.
elements.sent-2411-088: In our algorithms, we use regularized least-square estimator to estimate $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-089: For an agent $m \in [M]$, let $a_{m,1 , a_{m,2 , \ldots, a_{m,t}$ and $r_{m,1 , r_{m,2 , \ldots, r_{m,t}$ be the arms played and corresponding rewards obtained till time $t \in [T]$, we represent its local reward history by two local parameters $B_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$, where \begin{equation}\label{local_hist} B_{m,t} = \sum_{s=1}^t a_{m,s}a_{m,s}^{\top}, \text{ and } D_{m,t} = \sum_{s=1}^t a_{m,s}r_{m,s}.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-093: In this section, we describe our algorithm MaLinBAI-Star for star networks.
elements.sent-2411-094: Our learning model consists of a star network with a central server and $M$ agents.
elements.sent-2411-095: Each agent has a direct link to the central server.
elements.sent-2411-096: However, the setup assumes no direct links between the agents themselves.
elements.sent-2411-097: In other words, every agent in the network can directly communicate with the server but no two agents can communicate with each other.
elements.sent-2411-098: The pseudocode of MaLinBAI-Star is presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:star .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Given the time budget $T$, our algorithm partitions the budget into rounds of increasing length with each round $p$ lasting for $2^p$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-100: This allows frequent knowledge sharing in the early phases and hence facilitates more collaboration at the beginning of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-101: Throughout the execution of the algorithm, each agent $m$ maintains the local variables $B_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ as defined by Equation (\ref{local_hist ) to store its local reward history.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Additionally, the server also maintains the local variables $B_S$ and $D_S$, used for aggregating the information communicated by the agents.
elements.sent-2411-103: At the beginning of the first round i.e., at $t=1$, every agent $m$ constructs its confidence ellipsoid using Equation (\ref{ellipsoid ) and plays the action chosen according to Equation (\ref{select ).}}
elements.sent-2411-104: After observing the obtained reward $r_{m,t}$, the agent $m$ updates its local variables $B_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ and sends the updated variables to the server.
elements.sent-2411-105: The server then combines the local reward histories obtained from all agents and integrates them in its local variables $B_S$ and $D_S$ according to the Equation (\ref{server ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: Finally, the server sends its updated variables to the agents to be utilized in the next round.
elements.sent-2411-107: At this point, every agent also prepares itself for the next round by resetting its local variables to zero.
elements.sent-2411-108: Note that the server's local variables persist without reset, facilitating more informed arm selections by each agent in subsequent rounds and enabling the server to leverage the complete history of all agents when estimating the best arm.
elements.sent-2411-109: The server variables $B_S$ and $D_S$ are updated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{server} B_S = B_S + \sum_{m=1}^M B_{m,t} , \quad D_S = D_S + \sum_{m=1}^M D_{m,t}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{server} B_S = B_S + \sum_{m=1}^M B_{m,t} , \quad D_S = D_S + \sum_{m=1}^M D_{m,t}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-110: For all other rounds $p>1$, in every iteration $t$, the agents first calculate their local estimate of the unknown vector $\theta$ using the information received from the server in the previous round and their local reward history.
elements.sent-2411-111: Specifically, every agent $m$ calculates its local estimate $\hat{\theta _{m,t}$ using Equation (\ref{theta_estimate ), where the variables $V_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ are calculated as \begin{equation}\label{m_update} V_{m,t} = \lambda I + V_S + B_{m, t-1}, \quad D_{m,t} = D_S + D_{m, t-1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{m_update} V_{m,t} = \lambda I + V_S + B_{m, t-1}, \quad D_{m,t} = D_S + D_{m, t-1}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-112: Then, every agent $m$ constructs the confidence ellipsoid using its local estimate $\hat{\theta _{m,t}$ and plays the action chosen according to Equation (\ref{select ).}}
elements.sent-2411-113: At the end of the round $p$, the agent repeats the same procedure as the first round to update its local history and exchanging the updated information with the server.
elements.sent-2411-114: During this procedure, server also uses the same rule to aggregate the information received from the agents.
elements.sent-2411-115: At the end of the last round, the server receives the local reward histories from all the agents and updates its local variables $B_S$ and $D_S$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Subsequently, it computes an estimate of $\theta$ utilizing these variables.
elements.sent-2411-117: The server then determines the best arm by using the expression \begin{equation}a^* = \argmax_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left<\hat{\theta}_S, a \right>.\end{equation}.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{MaLinBAI-Star}\label{alg:star} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Arms set $\mathcal{A}$, budget $T$, number of agents $M$, regularization parameter $\lambda$, confidence parameter $\delta$ \State Initialization: $p \leftarrow 0, t \leftarrow 1, \tau \leftarrow 0, B_S \leftarrow 0, D_S \leftarrow 0, B_{m,t} \leftarrow 0, D_{m,t} \leftarrow 0 \quad \forall m \in [M]$ \While{$t \leq T$} \While{$t \le \min\{T, \tau + 2^p\}$} \For{$m = 1, \ldots, M$} \State update $V_{m,t}, D_{m,t}$ and $\hat{\theta}_{m,t}$ using (\ref{m_update}) and (\ref{theta_estimate}) \State construct $\mathcal{C}_{m,t}$ using (\ref{ellipsoid}) and select arm using (\ref{select}) \State Observe reward $r_{m,t}$ and update $B_{m,t}, D_{m,t}$ \State $B_{m,t} \leftarrow B_{m, t-1} + a_{m,t}a_{m,t}^{\top}$ \State $D_{m,t} \leftarrow D_{m, t-1} + a_{m,t}r_{m,t}$ \EndFor \State $t \leftarrow t + 1$ \EndWhile \State Send $B_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ to server and reset for next round \State Server updates its local variables using (\ref{server}) and sends back to agents \State $p \leftarrow p + 1$, $\tau \leftarrow t - 1$ \EndWhile \State Server estimates best arm \State $V_{S} \leftarrow \lambda I + B_S$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{S} \leftarrow V_{S}^{-1}D_{S}$ \State return $\argmax_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left<\hat{\theta}_S, a \right>$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-118: Communication cost: Our algorithm requires $O(\log T)$ rounds of communication between agents and server.
elements.sent-2411-119: Each round involves exchange of a data message between $M$ agents and the central server.
elements.sent-2411-120: Therefore, total communication cost incurred by our algorithm is $O(2M\log T)$.

elements.sent-2411-133: This section details our algorithm for generic networks.
elements.sent-2411-134: We consider multiple agents connected through a network represented by a graph $G = (V, E)$, where each vertex of $G$ represent an agent, and an edge between two vertices $u$ and $v$ represent the connection between the corresponding agents.
elements.sent-2411-135: Given such a graph $G$, our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen first partitions the graph into dominating sets.
elements.sent-2411-136: For this purpose, we first state following definitions: Dominating Set: Given a graph $G = (V, E)$, dominating set $D$ of $G$ is a subset of $V$ such that for any vertex $u \in V\setminus D$, there is a vertex $v \in D$ such that $v$ is connected to $u$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Dominating Set Partition: Given a graph $G$ and a dominating set $D$, the corresponding dominating set partition $P=\{P_1,\ldots,P_{|D|}\}$ is obtained by partitioning the vertices of $G$ in $|D|$ components such that each component contains exactly one vertex from $D$, known as the dominant vertex, and a subset of vertices directly connected to it by an edge (s0ee Figure~\ref{fig:network_structure ).}
elements.sent-2411-138: The pseudocode for our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen is presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:gen .}
elements.sent-2411-139: This algorithm is a simple extension of MaLinBAI-Star to generic networks.
elements.sent-2411-140: Given a network $G = (V, E)$ and a dominating set partition $P$ of $G$, each component $P_i$ in partition $P$ represents a star network, where the corresponding dominant vertex $p_i^h$ plays the role of central server or hub.
elements.sent-2411-141: Each of these component executes MaLinBAI-Star on the given bandit instance with the variation that the central hub $p_i^h$ also participates in the learning process by playing the actions and maintaining its local reward history.
elements.sent-2411-142: In this structure, there is a top-level ensembler which is responsible for estimating the best arm at the end of the budget.
elements.sent-2411-143: The ensembler directly communicates with the hub nodes of partition $P$.
elements.sent-2411-144: This configuration forms a hierarchical two-level star network as shown in Figure \ref{fig:network_structure .}
elements.sent-2411-145: At the first level, the ensembler is linked to $|P|$ hub nodes, each of which is further connected to a subset of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-146: Until the end of the budget, each component $P_i \in P$ executes MaLinBAI-Star independently where the hub nodes $p_i^h$ also play the actions and maintain their local reward history.
elements.sent-2411-147: At the end of the budget, the ensembler receives the local reward history from the hub node of each component in partition $P$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Then the ensembler integrates the received information using Equation (\ref{ensemble ).. \begin{equation}\label{ensemble} V_{E} \leftarrow \lambda I + \sum_{P_i \in P}B_{p_i^h}, \quad D_{E} \leftarrow \sum_{P_i \in P}D_{p_i^h}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Finally, the ensembler computes the estimate of theta using $V_E, D_E$ and declares the best arm using, \begin{equation}a^* = \argmax_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left<\hat{\theta}_E, a \right>.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-150: Communication cost: Execution of MaLinBAI-Star for a component $p_i \in P$ requires $O(2M_i\log T)$ communication cost where, $M_i$ is the number of agents present in component $p_i$ other than the dominant vertex $p_i^h$.
elements.sent-2411-151: MaLinBAI-Gen executes MaLinBAI-Star for each component $p_i \in P$ which takes total $O(\sum_{i=1 ^{|P| O(2M_i\log T)) = O(2(M - |P|)\log T)$ cost.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: At the end, all hub nodes send their local data to ensemble which adds $|P|$ to the cost.
elements.sent-2411-153: Therefore, the total communication cost required by MaLinBAI-Gen is $O(2(M - |P|)\log T + |P|)$.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{MaLinBAI-Gen}\label{alg:gen} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Arms set $\mathcal{A}$, budget $T$, a dominating set partition $P = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{|D|}\}$ on network graph $G = (V, E)$, regularization parameter $\lambda$, confidence parameter $\delta$ \State Initialization: $p \leftarrow 0, t \leftarrow 1, \tau \leftarrow 0, B_S \leftarrow 0, D_S \leftarrow 0, B_{m,t} \leftarrow 0, D_{m,t} \leftarrow 0 \quad \forall m \in V$ \While{$t \leq T$} \While{$t \le \min\{T, \tau + 2^p\}$} \For{$P_i \in P$} \For{$m \in P_i$} \State update $V_{m,t}, D_{m,t}$ and $\hat{\theta}_{m,t}$ using (\ref{m_update}) and (\ref{theta_estimate}) \State construct $\mathcal{C}_{m,t}$ using (\ref{ellipsoid}) and select arm using (\ref{select}) \State Observe reward $r_{m,t}$ and update $B_{m,t}, D_{m,t}$ \State $B_{m,t} \leftarrow B_{m, t-1} + a_{m,t}a_{m,t}^{\top}$ \State $D_{m,t} \leftarrow D_{m, t-1} + a_{m,t}r_{m,t}$ \EndFor \State $t \leftarrow t + 1$ \EndFor \EndWhile \If{$m$ is not the hub node} \State Send $B_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ to hub node $p_i^h$ and reset for next round \EndIf \State Hub updates its local variables using (\ref{server}) and sends back to agents \State $B_{p_i^h} \leftarrow B_{p_i^h} + \sum_{m\in P_i} B_{m,t}$ \State $D_{p_i^h} \leftarrow D_{p_i^h} + \sum_{m\in P_i} D_{m,t}$ \State $p \leftarrow p + 1$, $\tau \leftarrow t - 1$ \EndWhile \State Ensembler receives local reward history from all hub nodes and estimates the best arm \State $V_{E} \leftarrow \lambda I + \sum_{p_i \in P}B_{p_i^h}, \quad D_{S} \leftarrow \sum_{p_i \in P}D_{p_i^h}$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{E} \leftarrow V_{E}^{-1}D_{E}$ \State return $\argmax_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left<\hat{\theta}_E, a \right>$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-155: Let $a_1$ be the best arm and for any sub-optimal arm $a_o \in \mathcal{A}$, if $\mathcal{E _{a_o}$ is the event that MaLinBAI-Gen returns arm $a_o$ as the best arm then, probability of event $\mathcal{E _{a_o}$ is given by \begin{equation}\displaystyle\Pr[E_{a_o}] \leq 2e^2\exp\left\{-\frac{\left(\frac{2L\lambda\left(\lambda + MT/d - 1\right)\Delta_o}{\left|\left|a_1 - a_o\right|\right|_{V^{-1}}^2}\right)^2}{2T(MRL)^2}\right\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Given time budget $T$, the probability of error in estimating the best arm by MaLinBAI-Gen is given by \begin{equation}\Pr[Error] \leq (k-1)2e^2 \times\exp\left\{-\frac{\left(\frac{2L\lambda\left(\lambda + MT/d - 1\right)\Delta_{min}}{\max_{a \in A\setminus\{a_1\}}\left\|a_1 - a\right\|_{V^{-1}}^2}\right)^2}{2T(MRL)^2}\right\},\end{equation} where, $a_1$ is the best arm and $\Delta_{min}$ is the minimum sub-optimality gap of the given instance.
elements.sent-2411-157: Notice that, the error probability of MaLinBAI-Gen is the same as that of MaLinBAI-Star.
elements.sent-2411-158: This is due to the fact that, at the end, the amount of data used by the ensembler/server for estimating the best arm is the same for both algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-159: At any time $t > 0$, for every agent $m\in [M]$, true parameter value $\theta_*$ lies in the set \begin{equation}C_{m,t} = \displaystyle\left\{\theta \in R^d: \|\hat{\theta}_{m,t} - \theta\|_{V_{m,t}} \leq \sqrt{\lambda} W + \sqrt{d\log\left(\left(1 + MtL^2/\lambda d\right)/\delta\right)} \right\},\end{equation} with probability at least $(1 - \delta)$ for any $\delta > 0$.

elements.sent-2411-161: Remark: In Appendix D, we show that the error probability of our algorithms matches the lower bound in \cite{yang2022minimax up to a factor of $\log_2 d$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Additionally, the communication cost of our algorithms is shown to match the lower bound presented in \cite{he2022simple , up to a $(\log_2 T)^2$ factor difference.}
elements.sent-2411-163: As a result, our algorithms achieve near-optimal error rates while maintaining efficient communication costs.
elements.sent-2411-164: In this section, we compare the performance of our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen with several baselines including including (1) minimax optimal single-agent fixed-budget pure-exploration algorithm OD-LinBAI \cite{yang2022minimax , (2) multi-agent regret minimization algorithms FedLinUCB\cite{he2022simple , Async-LinUCB \cite{li2022asynchronous and, (3) multi-agent fixed-confidence best-arm identification algorithm FALinPE \cite{wang2023pure .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: We introduce a variant of OD-LinBAI, called MA-OD-LinBAI, to adapt its functionality for multi-agent environments.
elements.sent-2411-166: In this variant, with $M$ agents, MA-OD-LinBAI independently runs OD-LinBAI $M$ times, and the final best arm is selected as the one that appears most frequently among the $M$ best arms chosen by each agent.
elements.sent-2411-167: For regret-minimization algorithms, the best arm is identified as the arm pulled most frequently by the agents by the end of the allocated budget, with ties broken randomly.
elements.sent-2411-168: In the case of fixed-confidence algorithm FALinPE, we apply the stopping rule at the end of the budget to finalize the best arm.
elements.sent-2411-169: Finally, we run all the algorithms on the graph shown in Figure \ref{fig:net_exp , where the network is structured with a dominating set partition of size 5.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Note that our algorithm takes into account the topology of the network while the other algorithms just care about the number of agents in the network.
elements.sent-2411-171: We evaluate the algorithms on both the real-world MovieLens dataset \cite{harper2015movielens and three types of synthetic datasets: (1) Standard instance, (2) Random instance, and (3) Confounding instance.}
elements.sent-2411-172: For each of the instance, we execute the algorithms for time budget $150$ and number of agents $M = 15$.
elements.sent-2411-173: Noise is set as $\eta \sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$ generated independently for each trial.}
elements.sent-2411-174: We tune the parameter $\alpha$ for FedLinUCB and $\gamma_U=\gamma_D$ for Async-LinUCB, setting $\alpha=1$ and $\gamma_U=\gamma_D=5$ to match the optimal performance reported in the original papers.
elements.sent-2411-175: For FALinPE, we adopt the parameter values shown to achieve the lower bounds, specifically, $\gamma_1 = 1/M^2, \gamma_2 = 1/(2MK)$.
elements.sent-2411-176: The probability of error for each experiment is calculated as the fraction of errors made by the algorithms over 1000 Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-177: The standard error of error probabilities is represented by the tiny error bars in Figure \ref{comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-178: Due to space constraints, further discussion on numerical analysis and the results observed for real-world dataset are provided in Appendix E.
elements.sent-2411-179: Standard Instance: In this setup, the arm set consists of $d$-dimensional canonical vectors $\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_d\}$ with $d=10$.
elements.sent-2411-180: The unknown vector $\theta$ is set to $\Delta e_1$ where, $\Delta > 0$ thus, establishing first arm $e_1$ as the best arm.
elements.sent-2411-181: We execute the algorithms for $\Delta$ values ranging from $0.05$ to $0.5$, $\Delta = \{0.05, 0.1, \ldots, 0.5\}$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Random Instance: In this dataset, the arm set $\mathcal{A}$ consists of 100 random vectors drawn from the unit sphere in $\mathbb{R ^{d-1}$ centered at the origin.}
elements.sent-2411-183: To construct the unknown vector $\theta$, we choose two closest vectors from $\mathcal{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-184: Without loss of generality, let's assume that $u$ and $v$ are the two closest vectors in $\mathcal{A}$, we then define $\theta$ to be $u + 0.01(u - v)$, thereby designating $u$ as the optimal arm.
elements.sent-2411-185: Within this framework, we evaluate the algorithms for the dimension ranging from 5 to 40.
elements.sent-2411-186: Confounding Instance: In this scenario, the arm set is composed of $d$-dimensional canonical vectors ${e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_d}$ along with an additional arm $a_{d+1 = [\cos(\omega), \sin(\omega), 0, \ldots, 0]$.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Here, we designate $\theta = e_1$ to set $e_1$ as the optimal arm and by setting $\omega=0.1$, $a_{d+1}$ becomes the closest competing arm.
elements.sent-2411-188: For this instance, we execute the algorithms for values of $d$ ranging from 5 to 40.

elements.sent-2411-189: In Figure \ref{comparison , we observe that MaLinBAI-Gen consistently outperforms MA-OD-LinBAI, Async-LinUCB, FedLinUCB and, FALinPE across all datasets in terms of best arm identification.}
elements.sent-2411-190: In Appendix E, we present the numerical results on the communication cost required by each of the algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-191: These results show that, while the communication cost incurred by MaLinBAI-Gen is only marginally higher than that of Async-LinUCB and FedLinUCB, it is significantly lower than the cost required by FALinPE.
elements.sent-2411-192: However, none of these algorithms perform better than our algorithm MaLinBAI-Gen in accurately estimating the best arm.
elements.sent-2411-193: Thus, MaLinBAI-Gen strikes an effective balance by achieving both near-optimal error probability and efficient communication costs for federated linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-194: In this paper, we study the problem of fixed-budget, best-arm identification for stochastic linear bandits in a multi-agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-195: We devise two algorithms MaLinBAI-Star and MaLinBAI-Gen, which consider a network of agents connected through a star network or a generic network.
elements.sent-2411-196: The agents collaborate to learn the best arm with the help of a central server.
elements.sent-2411-197: MaLinBAI-Gen takes into account the structure of the network by using dominating set partitions.
elements.sent-2411-198: Both algorithms utilize the principle of optimism and achieve exponentially decaying error probability in the allocated time budget.
elements.sent-2411-199: As for future work, one could study a decentralized setting where agents need not be dependent on a central server for information sharing.
elements.sent-2411-200: Furthermore, designing a fault-tolerant, fully distributed algorithms is also a reasonable direction of study since in practice it is possible that agents may intermittently go offline.

elements.sent-2411-228: Then, for any agent $m \in [M]$, the local variables $V_{m,t}$ and $D_{m,t}$ at time $t$ can be represented as \begin{equation}V_{m,t} = \lambda I + V_S^{T} + B_{m,t-1} = \lambda I + \sum_{i=1}^M\sum_{s=1}^T a_{i,s}a_{i,s}^{\top} + \sum_{s=T+1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}a_{m,s}^{\top} = \lambda I + \sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}a_{m,s}^{\top} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}a_{i,s}^{\top}. \numberthis \label{vmt:star}\end{equation} \begin{equation}D_{m,t} = D_S^{T} + D_{m,t-1} = \sum_{i=1}^M\sum_{s=1}^T a_{i,s}r_{i,s} + \sum_{s=T+1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}r_{m,s} = \sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}r_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}r_{i,s}. \numberthis \label{dmt:star}\end{equation} Then, $\hat{\theta _{m,t}$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\hat{\theta}_{m,t} = V_{m,t}^{-1}D_{m,t} = V_{m,t}^{-1}\left[\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}r_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}r_{i,s}\right] = V_{m,t}^{-1}\left[\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}(a_{m,s}^{\top}\theta_* + \eta_{m,s}) + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}(a_{i,s}^{\top}\theta_* + \eta_{i,s})\right] = V_{m,t}^{-1}\left[\left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}a_{m,s}^{\top}\theta_* + \sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s}\right) + \left(\sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}a_{i,s}^{\top}\theta_* + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right)\right] = V_{m,t}^{-1}\left[\left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}a_{m,s}^{\top}\theta_* + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}a_{i,s}^{\top}\theta_* \right) + \left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right)\right] = V_{m,t}^{-1}\left[\left(V_{m,t} - \lambda I \right)\theta_* + \left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right)\right] = \theta_* - V_{m,t}^{-1}\lambda I \theta_* + V_{m,t}^{-1}\left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right) = \theta_* - V_{m,t}^{-1}\lambda I \theta_* + V_{m,t}^{-1}\left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right),\end{equation} Applying Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, \begin{equation}\left\|\hat{\theta}_{m,t} - \theta_* \right\|_{V_{m,t}} \leq \left\|V_{m,t}^{-1}\lambda I \theta_*\right\|_{V_{m,t}} + \left\|V_{m,t}^{-1}\left(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right)\right\|_{V_{m,t}} = \lambda \left\|\theta_*\right\|_{V_{m,t}^{-1}} + \left\|\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right\|_{V_{m,t}^{-1}},\end{equation} Using Theorem 1 of \cite{abbasi2011improved , \begin{equation}\left\|\sum_{s=1}^{t-1} a_{m,s}\eta_{m,s} + \sum_{i\in [M]\setminus\{m\}}\sum_{s=1}^{T} a_{i,s}\eta_{i,s}\right\|_{V_{m,t}^{-1}} \leq \sqrt{2R^2\log\left(\frac{\det(V_{m,t})^{1/2}\det(\lambda I)^{-1/2}}{\delta}\right)}, \label{A9}\end{equation} Since $V_{m,t}$ is positive definite, its eigenvalues are positive.}}

elements.sent-2411-294: In our learning model, we first consider multiple agents connected through a star network, interacting with a linear bandit instance in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-295: We then extend our analysis to arbitrary network topologies.
elements.sent-2411-296: The objective of the agents is to collaboratively identify the best arm of the given bandit instance with the help of a central server while minimizing the probability of error in best arm estimation.
elements.sent-2411-297: To this end, we propose two algorithms, MaLinBAI-Star and MaLinBAI-Gen for star networks and networks with arbitrary structure, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-298: Both algorithms utilize the technique of G-optimal design along with the successive elimination based strategy where agents share their knowledge through a central server at each communication round.
elements.sent-2411-299: We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our algorithms achieve exponentially decaying probability of error in the allocated time budget.
elements.sent-2411-300: Furthermore, experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data validate the effectiveness of our algorithms over the state-of-the art existing multi-agent algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-301: In this paper, we study fixed-budget best-arm identification in stochastic linear bandit (SLB) \cite{abbasi2011improved,auer2002using .}
elements.sent-2411-302: For example, in online advertising \cite{tao2018best , where each advertisement option can be modeled as an arm representing the properties like genre, keywords, duration and so on.}

elements.sent-2411-304: Given the learning network comprising multiple interconnected agents collaborating to complete a learning task, MaLinBAI-Gen first partitions the network into dominating sets.
elements.sent-2411-305: Each block of the dominating set partition executes MaLinBAI-Star independently where the corresponding dominant node plays the role of central server for that block in the partition.
elements.sent-2411-306: Later, the information gathered at these dominant nodes is aggregated by an ensemble using the majority vote to decide the best-arm of the given bandit instance.

elements.sent-2411-326: Formally, as defined in \cite{pukelsheim2006optimal , the problem of finding a G-optimal design seeks to find a probability distribution $\pi$ on the action set satisfying $\pi: \mathcal{A \rightarrow [0, 1]$ and $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A \pi(a) = 1$ that minimizes: \begin{equation}\label{g_opt} g(\pi) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}}\|a\|_{V_{(\pi)}^{-1}}^2 \text{, where } V_{(\pi)} = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a)aa^{\top}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-327: It is worth noticing that finding an exact solution to the G-optimal design problem is computationally hard.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{MaLinBAI-Star}\label{alg:star} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Arms set $\mathcal{A}$, budget $T$, number of agents $M$ \State Initialization: $\mathcal{A}_1 \leftarrow \mathcal{A}, b = \Big\lfloor \frac{T}{\lceil \log K\rceil}\Big\rfloor, V_S \leftarrow 0, D_S \leftarrow 0, V_{m} \leftarrow 0, D_{m} \leftarrow 0, \quad \forall m \in [M]$ \State Server assigns index and communicates the pair $(i, a_i), \forall a_i \in \mathcal{A}$ to all the agents \For{$p = 1$ to $\lceil \log K\rceil$} \State $d_r$ = rank($\mathcal{A}_r$) \State Project $\mathcal{A}_p$ to $d_p$ dimensions \State Server computes the distribution $\pi_p$ over $\mathcal{A}_p$ by solving G-optimal design in Equation (\ref{g_opt}) with support at most $\frac{d(d+1)}{2}$ \State Server sets $b_p(a_i) = b\pi_p(a_i)$ for all $a_i \in \mathcal{A}_r$ \For{all the agents $m \in [M]$} \State Server sends the pairs $(i, b_p(a_i)), \forall a_i \in \mathcal{A}_p$ to the agent $m$ \State Agent $m$ plays the arms following the values in the pairs $(i, b_p(a_i))$ and updates its local parameters $V_{m}, B_{m}$ using Equation (\ref{local_update}). \State Agent $m$ sends $V_{m}, B_{m}$ to the server and resets them for next round. \EndFor \State Server updates its local parameters $V_S$ and $B_S$ using Equation (\ref{server}) and computes the estimate $\hat{\theta}_S$ using Equation (\ref{theta_estimate}). \For{$a_i \in \mathcal{A}_p$} \State Server computes the estimated expected reward $x_p(a_i) = \hat{\theta}_S^{\top}a_i$. \EndFor \State Server computes the set $S_p$ of top $K/2^p$ arms with highest estimated expected reward and sets $\mathcal{A}_{p+1} = S_p$ \State Server resets $B_S$ and $D_S$. \EndFor \State Server outputs the only arm in $\mathcal{A}_{\lceil \log K\rceil}$ as the best arm. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-362: For this purpose, we first state following definitions: Dominating Set: Given a graph $G = (V, E)$, a dominating set $D$ of $G$ is a subset of $V$ such that for any vertex $u \in V\setminus D$, there is a vertex $v \in D$ such that $v$ is connected to $u$ by an edge.
elements.sent-2411-363: Dominating Set Partition: Given a graph $G$ and a dominating set $D$, the corresponding dominating set partition $P=\{P_1,\ldots,P_{|D|}\}$ is obtained by partitioning the vertices of $G$ into $|D|$ subsets (referred to as blocks or parts) such that each $P_i$ (with $1\leq i\leq |D|$) contains exactly one vertex from $v\in D$, known as the dominant vertex, and a subset of vertices directly connected to $v$ by an edge (see Figure~\ref{fig:network_structure ).}
elements.sent-2411-364: This algorithm is a natural extension of MaLinBAI-Star to arbitrary networks.
elements.sent-2411-365: Given a network $G = (V, E)$ and a dominating set partition $P$ of $G$, each block $P_i$ in the partition $P$ represents a star network, where the corresponding dominant vertex $p_i^h$ plays the role of central server or hub.
elements.sent-2411-366: So each block $P_i$ executes MaLinBAI-Star on the given bandit instance with the variation that the central hub $p_i^h$ also participates in the learning process by playing the actions and maintaining its local reward history.
elements.sent-2411-367: In this structure, there is a top-level server which is responsible for estimating the best arm at the end of the budget.
elements.sent-2411-368: At the first level, the server is linked to $|P|$ hub nodes, each of which is further connected to a subset of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-369: Until the end of the budget, each block $P_i$ executes MaLinBAI-Star independently where the hub nodes $p_i^h$ also play the actions and maintain their local reward history.
elements.sent-2411-370: At the end of the budget, the top-level server receives the estimated best arm from the hub node of each block in the partition $P$.
elements.sent-2411-371: Then the server determines the global best arm my using the majority vote mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-372: Specifically, the server selects the arm that appears most frequently among the $|P|$ best arms chosen by each hub node as the final best arm.
elements.sent-2411-373: In the event of a tie, i.e., multiple arms receiving the highest number of votes, the server determines the final best arm by selecting the arm with the lowest uncertainity in its estimated expected reward among the contending arms.
elements.sent-2411-374: For any arm $a$, this uncertainty is quantified using the expression $a^{\top V_p^{-1 a$, which reflects the confidence of each hub in its estimated expected reward for the selected arm.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{MaLinBAI-Gen}\label{alg:gen} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Arms set $\mathcal{A}$, budget $T$, a dominating set partition $P = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{|D|}\}$ on network graph $G = (V, E)$ \State Initialization: $\mathcal{A}_1 \leftarrow \mathcal{A}, b = \Big\lfloor \frac{T}{\lceil \log K\rceil}\Big\rfloor, V_S \leftarrow 0, D_S \leftarrow 0, V_{m} \leftarrow 0, D_{m} \leftarrow 0, \quad \forall m \in V$ \For{$P_i \in P$} \For{$p = 1$ to $\lceil \log K\rceil$} \State Execute lines (5)-(8) of MaLinBAI-Star \For{$m \in P_i$} \State Execute lines (10)-(12) of MaLinBAI-Star \EndFor \State Hub updates its local variables \State $V_{p_i^h} \leftarrow \sum_{m\in P_i} V_{m}$ \State $D_{p_i^h} \leftarrow \sum_{m\in P_i} D_{m}$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{p_i^h} = V_{p_i^h}^{-1}D_{p_i^h}$ \State Execute lines (15)-(19) of MaLinBAI-Star \EndFor \State Return the only arm in $\mathcal{A}_{\lceil \log K\rceil}$ and it's corresponding variance. \EndFor \State The server receives the estimated best arm and it's variance from all hub nodes \State Find most frequently chosen best arm $a_{out}$ among $|P|$ hub nodes \If{$a_{out}$ is unique} \State Return $a_{out}$ \Else{} \State Return the arm with lowest variance among possible values of $a_{out}$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-417: According to Theorem 3 of \cite{yang2022minimax , the lower bound on the probability of error in estimating the best arm is given by \begin{equation}\label{lower_bound} \exp\left(-O\left(\frac{T}{H_{1,lin(v)}\log_2 d}\right)\right),\end{equation} where $H_{1,lin(v) = \sum_{1 \leq i \leq d \Delta_i^{-2}$ denotes the hardness quantity of bandit instance $v$. \begin{equation}\sum_{1 \leq i \leq d} \Delta_i^{-2} \leq \sum_{1 \leq i \leq d} \Delta_{\min}^{-2} = \frac{d}{\Delta_{\min}^{2}}\end{equation} Therefore, quantity (\ref{lower_bound ) can be re-written as, \begin{equation}\exp\left(-O\left(\frac{T}{H_{1,lin(v)}\log_2 d}\right)\right) = \exp\left(-O\left(\frac{\Delta_{\min}^{2}T}{d\log d}\right)\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
